description: Calcifer feature backlog. harness.py verify writes verified_sha + verified_tree on success; harness.py complete sets passes=true. Other fields (title, priority, category, acceptance_criteria, verification, reference, description, motivation) are author-editable in plan commits only.

features.title: MCP auth token refresh callback
features.description: Allow long-running sessions to recover from expired MCP auth tokens via a user-provided refresh callback. Catches HTTPStatusError at the transport boundary (where httpx actually raises), not inside _send_request's JSON-RPC loop.
features.motivation: Sessions >30min that connect to OAuth-protected MCP servers currently die when the HTTP transport raises HTTPStatusError(401) from raise_for_status(). Claude Code's approach (claude.ts proxy fetch) is Anthropic-specific, so Calcifer invents a pluggable callback: the user provides a coroutine that takes the server name and returns fresh headers (or None to give up).
acceptance_criteria: MCPClient __init__ accepts on_auth_error: Callable[[str], Awaitable[dict|None]] | None = None
MCPTransport abstract class has update_headers(headers: dict) -> None; sse/http update, stdio no-ops
_send_request wraps transport.send() in try/except httpx.HTTPStatusError catching 401 and 403
On auth error with callback set and no prior retry: invoke callback, update headers, retry once
If callback returns None or is not set: raise the original HTTPStatusError
Callback exceptions are logged and the original auth error is raised
New test test_mcp_auth_refresh_callback_success — 401 then success
New test test_mcp_auth_refresh_callback_none — 401 with None return raises
New test test_mcp_auth_refresh_no_callback — 401 with no callback raises (baseline)
All existing mock tests still pass
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer.services.mcp.client import MCPClient; import inspect; sig = inspect.signature(MCPClient); assert 'on_auth_error' in sig.parameters, 'on_auth_error missing from MCPClient'"
.venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer.services.mcp.transport import MCPTransport; assert hasattr(MCPTransport, 'update_headers'), 'update_headers missing from MCPTransport'"
features.reference: No direct analog in Claude Code. claude-code-source/src/services/mcp/client.ts:363-421 (createClaudeAiProxyFetch) is Anthropic-specific (uses global getClaudeAIOAuthTokens + lockfile cache). Calcifer invents a generic callback pattern inspired by it but intentionally decoupled.

features.title: Stop hooks can inject messages and continue
features.description: Allow stop hooks to return a structured result: either stop the loop, or inject messages and continue. Backward-compatible with the existing bool return.
features.motivation: Claude Code's handleStopHooks returns blockingErrors that get injected as meta messages while the loop continues — used for budget warnings, content filter corrections, etc. Calcifer's stop hooks can only return bool (stop/not-stop). This limits extensibility for budget/policy hooks that want to nudge the model without killing the session.
acceptance_criteria: New StopHookResult dataclass in calcifer/agent.py with fields stop: bool and inject_messages: list[Message]
StopHookResult exported from calcifer/__init__.py
StopHookFn type accepts bool OR StopHookResult (sync or async)
Loop normalizes bool returns to StopHookResult internally
Injected messages appended to conversation (before next turn), marked is_meta=True
If any hook sets stop=True, loop breaks after all hooks run and injections applied
Hook exceptions are caught and logged (existing behavior preserved)
Injected meta messages do not break recovery.detect_interruption
New test test_stop_hook_inject_and_continue — inject without stopping
New test test_stop_hook_inject_and_stop — inject and then stop
Existing bool-based stop hook tests still pass
All 429 mock tests still pass
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer.agent import StopHookResult; r = StopHookResult(stop=False, inject_messages=[]); assert hasattr(r, 'stop') and hasattr(r, 'inject_messages')"

features.title: Abort signal with reason
features.description: Replace the bare asyncio.Event abort signal with a reason-aware signal distinguishing user interrupt from sibling error propagation.
features.motivation: Claude Code's AbortController carries a reason field (interrupt, sibling_error, streaming_fallback) used for debugging and synthetic error messages. Calcifer's single Event loses this distinction — all cancelled tools get the same opaque message.
acceptance_criteria: New AbortReason enum in calcifer/types/tools.py with values: USER_INTERRUPT, SIBLING_ERROR, TIMEOUT, OTHER
AbortReason exported from calcifer/__init__.py and calcifer/types/__init__.py
ToolContext.abort_reason field added (Optional[AbortReason], default None)
Agent.abort() accepts reason parameter (default USER_INTERRUPT)
Agent._abort_reason tracks the most recent abort reason
context.abort_reason propagates from agent state to tool context
Sibling-error cancellation sets SIBLING_ERROR on the tool result message
Cancelled tool result messages include reason text
New test test_abort_user_interrupt_reason
New test test_abort_sibling_error_reason
New test test_abort_default_reason
All 429 mock tests still pass
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer.types.tools import AbortReason; assert AbortReason.USER_INTERRUPT and AbortReason.SIBLING_ERROR"
.venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer.types.tools import ToolContext; import dataclasses; fields = {f.name for f in dataclasses.fields(ToolContext)}; assert 'abort_reason' in fields"
.venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer.agent import Agent; import inspect; sig = inspect.signature(Agent.abort); assert 'reason' in sig.parameters"
.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q -k 'abort_user_interrupt_reason or abort_sibling_error_reason or abort_default_reason'
features.reference: claude-code-source/src/services/tools/StreamingToolExecutor.ts:153-318 (abort reasons + sibling AbortController). claude-code-source/src/query.ts:1348-1351 (end-of-turn abort reason check).

features.title: Wire existing HookManager into tool orchestrator
features.description: HookEvent.PRE_TOOL_USE and POST_TOOL_USE plus HookManager.run_hooks already exist in calcifer/services/hooks.py, but the tool orchestrator never calls them. This feature wires hook invocation into execute_tool_call and StreamingToolExecutor.
features.motivation: The hook infrastructure (HookManager, HookConfig, HookResult with should_continue/updated_input/additional_context) is fully built but disconnected — the orchestrator has no hook calls. This is a low-effort, high-value wiring job: no new types, just connecting existing pieces. Enables audit logging, content filtering, input rewriting without touching tool code.
acceptance_criteria: ToolContext has an optional hook_manager: HookManager | None = None field
Agent passes self._hook_manager to every ToolContext it creates
execute_tool_call in orchestrator.py invokes hook_manager.run_hooks(PRE_TOOL_USE, ...) after check_input, before tool.call
If pre-hook returns should_continue=False: produce an error tool result with the stop_reason and skip tool.call
If pre-hook returns updated_input: re-validate and use the new input for tool.call
execute_tool_call invokes hook_manager.run_hooks(POST_TOOL_USE, ...) after tool.call, before context_modifier
Post-hook additional_context is appended to the tool result content
Hook exceptions are caught and logged (do not crash tool execution)
HookManager (not just the instance) is importable as calcifer.HookManager (already exported)
New test test_pre_tool_hook_veto — pre-hook stops execution
New test test_pre_tool_hook_rewrite — pre-hook mutates input
New test test_post_tool_hook_additional_context — post-hook appends text
New test test_hook_exception_does_not_crash
All 429 mock tests still pass
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer.types.tools import ToolContext; import dataclasses; assert 'hook_manager' in {f.name for f in dataclasses.fields(ToolContext)}"
.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q -k 'pre_tool_hook_veto or pre_tool_hook_rewrite or post_tool_hook_additional_context or tool_hook_exception_does_not_crash'
features.reference: calcifer/services/hooks.py:25-32,71+ (HookEvent, HookManager.run_hooks — already exist). claude-code-source/src/services/tools/toolExecution.ts:599-900 (runPreToolUseHooks, runPostToolUseHooks calling pattern).

features.title: Skill when_to_use frontmatter field
features.description: Add when_to_use frontmatter field to skill definitions, surfaced in the skill list prompt so the LLM knows when to invoke each skill.
features.motivation: Claude Code skills have a when_to_use field that tells the model when each skill is relevant. Calcifer currently only has description, which is often too terse. Adding when_to_use improves skill selection without adding a new concept. Canary feature — small and isolated, good first workflow run.
acceptance_criteria: SkillDefinition has a when_to_use field (str, default empty)
load_skill_file parses both when-to-use and when_to_use from frontmatter
apply_token_budget includes when_to_use in the entry when present
Token char count includes when_to_use length
Existing skills without when_to_use still load correctly
New test test_skill_when_to_use_parsing
New test test_skill_budget_includes_when_to_use
Existing skill tests still pass
All 429 mock tests still pass
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer.skills.loader import SkillDefinition; import dataclasses; assert 'when_to_use' in {f.name for f in dataclasses.fields(SkillDefinition)}"
features.review_notes: Approved (high confidence, fresh-context subagent review). Symbol collision check clean: SkillDefinition at calcifer/skills/loader.py:28 has no when_to_use field, no parser wiring, zero grep hits in calcifer/ and tests/. Design matches source reality (known_keys set at loader.py:80, apply_token_budget snippet at loader.py:203 matches contract verbatim). Verification gate is real: field-check and -k when_to_use both fail before impl, pass after. Two non-blocking nits: (a) contract says '429 mock tests' baseline but actual is 451 — regression command runs whole suite so drift is inert; (b) contract claims upstream accepts 'camelCase or snake_case' for when_to_use but loadSkillsDir.ts:252 only reads frontmatter.when_to_use (snake_case) — Calcifer's both-keys parsing is a legitimate extension but the upstream claim is slightly inaccurate. Generator note: ensure the two new tests assert on parsed VALUE and budget output substring, not just field existence, to keep rule 8 footgun closed.

features.title: Contract review mechanism (gate verify on approved review)
features.description: Add harness.py review <id> and harness.py review-record <id> subcommands. Review generates a packet (contract + features.json entry + machine sanity results + checklist) for an external reviewer (human or subagent) to evaluate. review-record stores the verdict in features.json with SHA-based locking. verify and complete refuse to run unless review_status == 'approved' and the contract hash matches the reviewed one.
features.motivation: Currently the harness has no gate between 'contract written' and 'implementation started'. This caused round-1 reviewer to catch a live instance of the problem: the original pre-post-tool-hooks contract proposed adding HookEvent.PRE_TOOL_USE, which already existed — the author wrote the contract without reading the source. A contract review step would have caught this in minutes instead of after implementation. Anthropic's harness-design-long-running-apps article explicitly makes this step the core mechanic: 'Before each sprint, the generator and evaluator negotiated a sprint contract: agreeing on what "done" looked like for that chunk of work before any code was written.'
acceptance_criteria: harness.py review <id> prints a structured review packet to stdout (contract text, features.json entry, machine sanity results, reviewer checklist, verdict instructions)

harness.py review-record <id> --status {approved|changes_requested|blocking} --notes TEXT updates features.json fields: review_status, review_notes, reviewed_at (ISO timestamp), reviewed_contract_sha (sha256 of contract file bytes)
review-record REJECTS an approved status if the contract doesn't exist or has unresolved TODOs (the one structural gate that's non-negotiable)
review-record --notes TEXT requires non-empty notes for any non-approved status (justify rejection)
New Feature dataclass fields: review_status (default ''), review_notes (default ''), reviewed_at (default ''), reviewed_contract_sha (default '')
cmd_verify refuses to run if review_status != 'approved' (with clear error + suggested command)
cmd_verify refuses to run if current contract sha != reviewed_contract_sha (contract edited since review — must re-review)
Gate escape: --skip-review REASON flag on verify and complete, requires non-empty audit reason, printed to stderr
cmd_reset clears all four review fields alongside verified_sha/verified_tree

Review packet includes: contract file path, full contract text, features.json entry as pretty JSON, list of files mentioned in contract (with exists/missing marker), list of verification commands with validator result, a 10-point checklist for the reviewer

Machine sanity in the packet: (a) every file path mentioned exists OR is clearly a new-file path; (b) every verification command passes validate_and_parse_verify_command; (c) contract has non-empty Motivation/Scope/Design/Acceptance Criteria/Verification Commands sections; (d) no literal 'TODO:' markers remain
New test test_review_record_approves_and_gates
New test test_review_record_rejects_without_notes
New test test_review_record_detects_contract_edit
New test test_verify_refuses_without_approved_review
New test test_skip_review_with_reason
All 434 existing mock tests still pass
progress.md documents the calibration philosophy (article quote + plan for iterating on reviewer prompts)
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "import subprocess; r = subprocess.run(['.venv/bin/python', 'harness/harness.py', 'review', '--help'], capture_output=True, text=True); assert r.returncode == 0 and 'review' in r.stdout, r.stdout + r.stderr"

.venv/bin/python -c "import subprocess; r = subprocess.run(['.venv/bin/python', 'harness/harness.py', 'review-record', '--help'], capture_output=True, text=True); assert r.returncode == 0 and '--status' in r.stdout, r.stdout + r.stderr"

.venv/bin/python -c "import sys; sys.path.insert(0, 'harness'); import harness as h; assert 'review_status' in {f.name for f in __import__('dataclasses').fields(h.Feature)}, 'Feature missing review_status field'"

.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q -k 'review_record_approves_and_gates or review_record_rejects_without_notes or review_record_detects_contract_edit or verify_refuses_without_approved_review or skip_review_with_reason or reset_clears_review_fields'
features.reference: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/harness-design-long-running-apps — specifically the 'sprint contract' negotiation pattern: 'Before each sprint, the generator and evaluator negotiated a sprint contract... The two iterated until they agreed.' Our implementation replaces the human evaluator role with a subagent call driven by the caller (not spawned by harness.py itself).

features.title: Add py.typed marker for type checking
features.description: Create calcifer/py.typed (PEP 561 marker) and declare package-data in pyproject.toml so downstream users get type checking when they import calcifer.
features.motivation: Without py.typed, mypy/pyright treat calcifer as an untyped library (every type becomes Any). The first thing an SDK user does is import and type-check; this is the minimum bar.
acceptance_criteria: File calcifer/py.typed exists (can be empty)
pyproject.toml includes py.typed in package-data via [tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
From a fresh clone, `.venv/bin/python -c 'import calcifer; from pathlib import Path; print((Path(calcifer.__file__).parent / "py.typed").exists())'` prints True
All 434 mock tests still pass
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from pathlib import Path; import calcifer; p = Path(calcifer.__file__).parent / 'py.typed'; assert p.exists(), f'py.typed missing at {p}'"
features.reference: PEP 561 — https://peps.python.org/pep-0561/ (no Claude Code analog — purely Python packaging convention)
features.review_notes: Approved (high confidence). Verified: calcifer/py.typed does not exist, tests/test_packaging.py does not exist, pyproject.toml has no [tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel] section. Hatchling build backend confirmed at pyproject.toml:38-40. Reviewer flagged one footgun (rule 8): a lazy impl could ship just py.typed without the pyproject toml stanza and pass the runtime-path check. Generator note: add a tomllib-based assertion in test_packaging.py that verifies pyproject.toml declares py.typed via [tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel] packages or force-include.

features.title: Remove hardcoded base_url, read from OPENAI_BASE_URL env
features.description: Change CalciferConfig.base_url default from 'http://127.0.0.1:8317/v1' to None. At runtime, if None, use os.environ.get('OPENAI_BASE_URL', 'https://api.openai.com/v1').
features.motivation: The current default is a private local gateway address that makes no sense for SDK users. Standard SDK behavior is to read from env var with OpenAI's public URL as the fallback.
acceptance_criteria: CalciferConfig.base_url default is None (or Optional[str])
Agent resolves the effective base_url at init time: config.base_url -> env OPENAI_BASE_URL -> 'https://api.openai.com/v1'
No reference to '127.0.0.1:8317' in calcifer/ source
New test test_config_base_url_env_fallback verifies env resolution
New test test_config_base_url_explicit verifies explicit value wins over env
All existing tests still pass (update any that relied on the old default)
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer.config import CalciferConfig; import dataclasses; f = next(x for x in dataclasses.fields(CalciferConfig) if x.name == 'base_url'); assert f.default is None, f'base_url default should be None, got {f.default!r}'"

.venv/bin/python -c "import subprocess; r = subprocess.run(['grep', '-rn', '127.0.0.1:8317', 'calcifer/'], capture_output=True, text=True); assert r.returncode != 0 or not r.stdout.strip(), f'127.0.0.1:8317 still in calcifer/: {r.stdout}'"
.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q -k 'config_base_url_explicit_wins or config_base_url_env_fallback or config_base_url_canonical_fallback'
features.reference: Standard 12-factor config. No Claude Code analog.
features.review_notes: Approved (high confidence). Cross-checked all 4 line numbers (config.py:27, agent.py:81, provider.py:74, executor.py:56) — exact match with current source. Verified _resolve_base_url and OPENAI_BASE_URL don't exist anywhere in calcifer/. Non-goal of leaving LLMProvider default as a literal (not None+resolver) is coherent — resolver belongs at the public Agent surface, transport should fail loudly on None. Generator note: write resolved value back to self._config.base_url BEFORE the LLMProvider(...) call at agent.py:114 so the provider receives a real string.

features.title: Add Agent.run_sync() synchronous wrapper
features.description: Add a thin synchronous wrapper method Agent.run_sync(prompt, messages=None) that runs the async .run() via asyncio.run().
features.motivation: Async-only agents are friction for users who just want to call the agent from a sync script or CLI. A 5-line wrapper solves this without rewriting anything. SDK users expect both.
acceptance_criteria: Agent.run_sync(prompt, *, messages=None) -> AgentResult method exists
run_sync raises if called from within an already-running asyncio loop (clear error, no silent breakage)
New test test_agent_run_sync_basic: mock provider, sync call returns AgentResult
New test test_agent_run_sync_inside_loop_raises
All 434 existing tests still pass
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from calcifer import Agent; import inspect; assert hasattr(Agent, 'run_sync'), 'run_sync missing'; assert not inspect.iscoroutinefunction(Agent.run_sync), 'run_sync should be sync'"
features.reference: Standard asyncio.run wrapping pattern; Anthropic SDK does similar.
features.review_notes: Approved high confidence. Verified run_sync doesn't exist, asyncio already imported (agent.py:19), run defined at agent.py:384. test_p0.py doesn't have the proposed test names. Reviewer confirmed asyncio.get_running_loop() is the canonical detection idiom. final_text assertion in test_agent_run_sync_basic neutralizes the 'return coroutine unawaited' footgun.

features.title: Full PyPI metadata in pyproject.toml
features.description: Fill in pyproject.toml with: description, authors, license, readme, homepage, repository, keywords, classifiers (development status, python versions, license, topic), project.urls. Bump version to 0.3.0-dev.
features.motivation: Current pyproject.toml has only name/version/deps. PyPI rejects or poorly lists packages without metadata. This is a prerequisite for publishing.
acceptance_criteria: pyproject.toml has [project] with: name, version, description, readme, license, authors, requires-python, keywords, classifiers, urls (Homepage, Repository, Issues, Changelog)
version is '0.3.0.dev0' (PEP 440 pre-release)

classifiers include at minimum: 'Development Status :: 3 - Alpha', 'Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11', '3.12', '3.13', 'License :: OSI Approved :: MIT License', 'Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries'
README.md is referenced via readme field
LICENSE file exists at repo root (MIT)
`.venv/bin/python -m build` (or equivalent) produces a valid sdist + wheel without errors
All 434 tests still pass
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "import tomllib; from pathlib import Path; d = tomllib.loads(Path('pyproject.toml').read_text()); p = d['project']; assert p['version'] == '0.3.0.dev0', f'version is {p[\"version\"]}'; assert p['license'], 'license missing'; assert p.get('authors'), 'authors missing'; assert p.get('keywords'), 'keywords missing'; assert p.get('classifiers'), 'classifiers missing'; assert p.get('readme'), 'readme missing'; assert d['project'].get('urls'), 'project.urls missing'"
.venv/bin/python -c "from pathlib import Path; p = Path('LICENSE'); assert p.exists(), 'LICENSE file missing'; assert p.stat().st_size >= 500, f'LICENSE is too small: {p.stat().st_size} bytes'"
features.review_notes: Approved (high confidence). Verified pyproject.toml currently has only name/version/description/requires-python/dependencies (lines 1-10), version still 0.2.0. LICENSE file does not exist. tests/test_packaging.py only has the 2 tests from sdk-py-typed-marker. All 3 verify gates fail cleanly pre-impl. Generator note: use license={text='MIT'} (table form) not the SPDX string — hatchling version is unpinned in pyproject.toml line 39 and PEP 639 SPDX strings need hatchling >= 1.26 which we can't guarantee. Minor nit: cmd1 only checks d['project'].get('urls') truthiness, the four-key check happens via the pytest test, which is fine.

features.title: Introduce calcifer/core/ subpackage
features.description: Create calcifer/core/ and move the core modules (agent.py, tool.py, config.py, types/ become core/types.py, services/api/provider.py -> core/provider.py, services/compact/context.py -> core/context.py, services/tools/orchestrator.py -> core/orchestrator.py, services/hooks.py -> core/hooks.py) into it. Keep top-level re-exports so `from calcifer import Agent` still works.
features.motivation: Flat calcifer/services/ mixes core and extensions. Moving core into a dedicated subpackage clarifies 'this is the minimal SDK' vs 'this is optional'. Preparatory refactor for later product-package splits.

features.title: Introduce calcifer/ext/ for optional subsystems

features.title: Rename calcifer/tools/ to calcifer/builtins/
features.description: Rename calcifer/tools/ -> calcifer/builtins/ to avoid naming conflict with the Tool class concept. Update imports in tool_registry.py and anywhere else.
features.motivation: calcifer.tools is confusing — tools ARE the concept, and calcifer/tools/ contains the built-in implementations. builtins/ is clearer. Also avoids any collision with the Tool type.

features.title: Add calcifer/testing/ module with MockProvider and assertions
features.description: Create calcifer/testing/ with: MockProvider (LLMProvider replacement that returns canned responses + tool calls), assertion helpers (assert_tool_called, assert_message_count), test fixtures.
features.motivation: SDK users need to test their agents without hitting real LLMs. Without a MockProvider, every user has to invent their own. Providing it is standard SDK courtesy.

features.title: Split calcifer/tui/ into calcifer-tui package
features.description: Create a sibling directory calcifer-tui/ as a new Python package that depends on calcifer>=0.3.0 and rich>=13. Move calcifer/tui/ contents there. Remove the [tui] optional extra from calcifer/pyproject.toml.
features.motivation: TUI is a product, not an SDK. Users of the SDK shouldn't have to evaluate rich as a transitive dep. Keeping TUI in-tree blocks clean SDK publishing.
acceptance_criteria: New directory calcifer-tui/ at repo root with its own pyproject.toml
calcifer-tui/ package name is 'calcifer-tui', imports as 'calcifer_tui'
calcifer/tui/ removed from calcifer package
calcifer-tui/ depends on calcifer>=0.3.0.dev0 and rich>=13, prompt-toolkit>=3
tests/test_tui_web.py (TUI parts) moved to calcifer-tui/tests/
Main calcifer pyproject no longer has [tui] extra
Both packages build as sdist+wheel without errors
Mock tests in main package still pass (tests/test_tui_web.py removed or moved)

features.title: Split calcifer/web/ into calcifer-web package
features.description: Same pattern as TUI split: calcifer-web/ sibling package with fastapi + uvicorn deps.
features.motivation: Web GUI pulls in fastapi/uvicorn which are heavy. SDK users shouldn't see them.
acceptance_criteria: calcifer-web/ directory with own pyproject.toml
Package imports as 'calcifer_web'
calcifer/web/ removed from main package
Web tests moved to calcifer-web/tests/
Main pyproject no longer has [web] extra
Both packages build cleanly
Main mock tests still pass

features.title: Split calcifer/cli.py into calcifer-cli package
features.description: Move calcifer/cli.py and calcifer/__main__.py to a calcifer-cli/ sibling package. The `calcifer` command-line entry point lives there, depends on calcifer + calcifer-tui + calcifer-web.
features.motivation: The CLI glues together core + TUI + Web + print mode. It's a product. Shipping it with the core SDK means SDK users can't install without the full stack.
acceptance_criteria: calcifer-cli/ directory with own pyproject.toml
Entry point 'calcifer = calcifer_cli:main' defined
calcifer/cli.py and calcifer/__main__.py removed from main package
calcifer-cli depends on calcifer, calcifer-tui, calcifer-web
CLI tests moved to calcifer-cli/tests/
Main pyproject.scripts no longer defines 'calcifer'
`.venv/bin/calcifer --help` works after installing calcifer-cli
Main mock tests still pass

features.title: Set up docs/ directory with getting-started + architecture
features.description: Create docs/ with: index.md (overview), getting-started.md (3-line hello world + tool example), architecture.md (6-layer compaction, agent loop, tool execution pipeline), public-api.md (from sdk-public-api-audit).
features.motivation: Can't publish an SDK without docs. This sets up the structure; the cookbook feature adds concrete examples.
acceptance_criteria: docs/ directory exists at repo root
docs/index.md — overview + links to other docs
docs/getting-started.md — install instructions + hello world + first tool example (runnable)
docs/architecture.md — agent loop diagram, compaction pipeline, tool execution flow
docs/public-api.md — exists (may be filled by sdk-public-api-audit)
Each doc has concrete code examples that import from calcifer
README.md has a 'Documentation' section linking to docs/

features.title: Cookbook: concrete examples for common SDK patterns
features.description: Create docs/cookbook/ with runnable example scripts: custom-tool.md, mcp-integration.md, session-resume.md, multi-agent.md, streaming-ui.md, testing-agents.md.
features.motivation: Getting-started shows the 80% case. Cookbook shows how to do the specific things users actually ask about. Without cookbook, users file 'how do I...' issues instead of using the library.
acceptance_criteria: docs/cookbook/ directory exists
At least 5 cookbook files each with a runnable code example
Examples cover: custom tool via @tool, MCP server connection, session save/resume, multi-agent with Coordinator, streaming agent events to a UI, testing with MockProvider
Each example imports only from calcifer public API
examples/ directory at repo root has the runnable .py versions

features.title: CHANGELOG.md and semver policy document
features.description: Create CHANGELOG.md (keep-a-changelog format) with 0.3.0-dev placeholder, and docs/semver.md explaining what constitutes a breaking change.
features.motivation: Publishing without a changelog is amateurish. Users need to know what changed between versions. Semver policy prevents 'we broke your code in a patch release' surprises.
acceptance_criteria: CHANGELOG.md at repo root in keep-a-changelog format
Unreleased section describes all changes since v0.2.0
docs/semver.md defines: what triggers major/minor/patch, what's 'public API' (references sdk-public-api-audit), deprecation policy (one minor version warning minimum)
README.md links to CHANGELOG and semver policy
verification: .venv/bin/python -c "from pathlib import Path; p = Path('CHANGELOG.md'); assert p.exists(), 'CHANGELOG.md missing'; t = p.read_text(); assert '## [0.3.0]' in t, 'CHANGELOG missing v0.3.0 section'; assert 'keepachangelog' in t.lower() or 'keep a changelog' in t.lower(), 'CHANGELOG header should reference keep-a-changelog'"

.venv/bin/python -c "from pathlib import Path; p = Path('docs/semver.md'); assert p.exists(), 'docs/semver.md missing'; t = p.read_text(); assert 'public-api.md' in t, 'semver.md should reference public-api.md'; assert 'Major' in t and 'Minor' in t and 'Patch' in t, 'semver.md missing version-bump triggers'"
features.review_notes: Round 2 approved: scope now trims public-api.md's semver/propose sections to one-line pointers; test spec enforces >=5 bullets across >=2 distinct ### categories matching AC.

features.title: GitHub Actions CI: test matrix + build + TestPyPI
features.description: Create .github/workflows/ with: ci.yml (test matrix 3.11/3.12/3.13 on ubuntu+macos, lint if configured, run mock tests), publish.yml (on tag v*, build sdist+wheel, publish to TestPyPI then PyPI with OIDC).
features.motivation: Manual publishing is error-prone and not reproducible. Tag-triggered publish via OIDC is the current best practice.
acceptance_criteria: .github/workflows/ci.yml exists and runs on push/PR
CI matrix: python {3.11, 3.12, 3.13} x os {ubuntu-latest, macos-latest}
CI step: install .venv, run mock tests (ignoring e2e + tui_web)
.github/workflows/publish.yml exists, triggered on tag v*
publish.yml uses python-build and pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish
OIDC configured (no API keys in repo)
Both workflows valid YAML (actionlint clean if available)
README has CI badge

features.title: Publish v0.3.0 to PyPI
features.description: Tag v0.3.0, push tag, verify GitHub Actions publishes to TestPyPI, install from TestPyPI in a fresh venv, smoke-test, then trigger PyPI publish. Bump version to 0.3.1.dev0 afterward.
features.motivation: This is the actual shipping step. Everything above is preparation.
acceptance_criteria: pyproject.toml version set to '0.3.0' (no .dev suffix)
CHANGELOG.md has a real [0.3.0] section with date
Git tag v0.3.0 created and pushed
GitHub Actions successfully publishes to TestPyPI
Fresh venv: `pip install -i https://test.pypi.org/simple/ calcifer==0.3.0` succeeds
Smoke test: `python -c 'from calcifer import Agent; print(Agent.__doc__)'` works
GitHub Actions successfully publishes to PyPI (after maintainer approval)
Version bumped to 0.3.1.dev0 in a post-release commit
